content.text: Hybrid retrieval runs two or more search systems in parallel and produces independent ranked lists. Fusion is the step that merges those lists into a single, better-ranked output. The choice of fusion algorithm determines how much each retriever's evidence is weighted, whether the overlap of results across retrievers is rewarded, and how robust the merge is to outlier scores.
There are three families of fusion strategy, ordered roughly by cost and quality:
only the position in each list matters (RRF, Borda count). No score normalisation required.
normalised retriever scores are linearly combined (CombSUM, CombMNZ, weighted blend).
a trained model (cross-encoder, LTR) scores each candidate from scratch.

attrs.alt: Three-column comparison of fusion strategies: rank-based (RRF, Borda), score-based (CombSUM, CombMNZ), and learning-based (LTR, cross-encoder reranker), with properties and a production pipeline flow at the bottom.

content.text: Rank-based methods ignore the actual scores returned by each retriever and use only the positions of documents in each ranked list. This makes them robust to any mismatch in score magnitude or distribution between retrievers.
RRF was introduced by Cormack, Clarke, and Buettcher (2009) and has become the de-facto default for hybrid search. Each document's score is the sum of reciprocal ranks across all lists:
is the set of ranked lists,
is a smoothing constant (default 60). Documents not in a list contribute 0 for that list.

from collections import defaultdict

def rrf(ranked_lists: list[list[str]], k: int = 60) -> list[tuple[str, float]]:
    """Merge ranked lists using Reciprocal Rank Fusion.

    Args:
        ranked_lists: Each inner list is a ranked sequence of document IDs,
                      best first (index 0 = rank 1).
        k: Smoothing constant (60 is the standard default).

    Returns:
        List of (doc_id, rrf_score) sorted by score descending.
    """
    scores: dict[str, float] = defaultdict(float)
    for ranked in ranked_lists:
        for rank, doc_id in enumerate(ranked, start=1):
            scores[doc_id] += 1.0 / (k + rank)
    return sorted(scores.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)

# Example
bm25_results = ["doc-3", "doc-1", "doc-7", "doc-12"]
dense_results = ["doc-9", "doc-3", "doc-22", "doc-5"]

merged = rrf([bm25_results, dense_results], k=60)
for doc_id, score in merged[:5]:
    print(f"{doc_id:10s}  {score:.5f}")
# doc-3      0.03279   ← in both lists (rank 1 BM25, rank 2 dense)
# doc-9      0.01613   ← only in dense (rank 1)
# doc-1      0.01613   ← only in BM25 (rank 2)
# doc-7      0.01572   ← only in BM25 (rank 3)
# doc-22     0.01572   ← only in dense (rank 3)
Effect of k on ranking sensitivity:
rank differences between positions 1 and 5 are amplified; the top-1 document dominates. Better when one retriever is known to be highly precise at rank 1.
the score curve is flatter; results spread more evenly across the full ranked list. Better when recall is the priority.
the original paper's value — empirically robust across most retrieval settings. Start here; tune only with labelled data.
RRF with more than two retrievers:

sum the reciprocal rank terms from every list. Adding a third retriever (e.g. a keyword matcher for exact codes) increases the overlap bonus and usually helps. Lists with no result for a document just contribute 0.
Borda count assigns each document a vote equal to the number of lower-ranked documents below it:
is the number of candidates in list
. A document at rank 1 in a list of 100 scores 99 from that list; at rank 100 it scores 0. Documents absent from a list score 0 from it.
Borda is slightly simpler than RRF but behaves similarly in practice. RRF is generally preferred because its
decay is smoother and the smoothing constant provides an explicit tuning knob.

Score-based methods normalise each retriever's raw scores to a common range, then linearly combine them. They preserve within-retriever score granularity — a document at rank 1 with score 50 vs rank 1 with score 10 from BM25 are treated differently — which can help when the retriever is well-calibrated.

Raw BM25 scores and cosine distances are on incompatible scales. Normalisation must happen per-query, because BM25 scores depend on the corpus TF-IDF statistics for the specific query terms, and ANN distances depend on the query vector's neighbourhood.
if one document has a very high BM25 score (e.g. exact match in a short corpus), it drives
up and compresses all other scores toward 0. Z-score normalisation is more robust to such outliers.
After normalisation, CombSUM simply adds the normalised scores across all retrievers. For absent documents the contribution from that retriever is 0:
is the normalised score from retriever
CombMNZ multiplies the CombSUM score by the number of retrievers that returned the document. This explicitly rewards documents that appear in multiple lists — the

CombMNZ typically outperforms CombSUM when the retrieval signals are complementary (BM25 and dense), because the overlap bonus acts as a quality signal: a document that both retrievers independently consider relevant is a strong candidate.
When you have labelled data and want to control the balance explicitly:
When highest-quality fusion is required and latency budget allows, a trained model can score each candidate more accurately than any unsupervised formula.

A cross-encoder takes the concatenation of the query and a single document and produces a relevance score. Unlike bi-encoders, cross-encoders see the full query–document interaction, allowing them to capture fine-grained term overlap, question–answer alignment, and negation. This makes them significantly more accurate but also O(n) in cost for each query.

from sentence_transformers import CrossEncoder

# ms-marco-MiniLM-L-12-v2 is a commonly used reranker (~33M params)
reranker = CrossEncoder("cross-encoder/ms-marco-MiniLM-L-12-v2", max_length=512)

def cross_encoder_rerank(
    query: str,
    candidate_docs: list[dict],   # each has "id" and "text"
    top_n: int = 10,
) -> list[dict]:
    pairs = [(query, doc["text"]) for doc in candidate_docs]
    scores = reranker.predict(pairs)            # returns numpy array
    ranked = sorted(
        zip(candidate_docs, scores),
        key=lambda x: x[1],
        reverse=True,
    )
    return [doc for doc, _ in ranked[:top_n]]

# Typical pipeline: fuse top-50 from BM25+dense with RRF, rerank to top-10
fused_top50 = rrf([bm25_ids[:50], dense_ids[:50]])[:50]
candidates = [{"id": doc_id, "text": corpus[doc_id]} for doc_id, _ in fused_top50]
final_top10 = cross_encoder_rerank(query, candidates, top_n=10)
Cross-encoder latency scales with the number of candidates and document length. Keeping the candidate set to 20–50 documents (produced by fast RRF) holds reranker latency to 50–200ms on a single GPU.

ColBERT is a middle ground: the query and document are both encoded into token-level matrices rather than single vectors. Relevance is the MaxSim aggregation of these matrices, allowing fine-grained interaction without full cross-attention:

Pre-computed document token matrices live in a compressed index; only the query is encoded at run-time. This gives ColBERT quality close to cross-encoders at much lower latency (typically 10–50ms for re-ranking 1000 documents).

# ColBERT v2 via RAGATOUILLE (simple wrapper)
from ragatouille import RAGPretrainedModel

colbert = RAGPretrainedModel.from_pretrained("colbert-ir/colbertv2.0")
colbert.index(
    collection=[doc["text"] for doc in corpus],
    index_name="my_corpus",
    max_document_length=256,
)

# Retrieve + rerank in one call
results = colbert.search(
    query="transformer scaled dot-product attention",
    k=10,
)
for r in results:
    print(r["score"], r["content"][:80])

LTR trains a model to predict relevance from a hand-crafted feature vector. Each candidate document is represented by features such as BM25 score, dense cosine distance, document length, recency, and click rate. A gradient-boosted tree (LambdaMART, XGBoost) or a small neural network maps the feature vector to a relevance score.

import numpy as np
from sklearn.ensemble import GradientBoostingRegressor

# Feature extraction for a (query, doc) pair
def build_features(query: str, doc: dict, bm25_score: float, dense_score: float) -> np.ndarray:
    return np.array([
        bm25_score,
        dense_score,
        len(doc["text"].split()),          # doc length
        bm25_score / (dense_score + 1e-9), # score ratio
        int(query.lower() in doc["text"].lower()),  # exact query match
    ])

# Training (offline, with human relevance labels 0–3)
X_train = np.array([build_features(*row) for row in train_data])
y_train = np.array([row["relevance"] for row in train_data])

ltr_model = GradientBoostingRegressor(n_estimators=200, max_depth=4)
ltr_model.fit(X_train, y_train)

# At query time: re-score the fused candidates
def ltr_rerank(query, candidates, bm25_scores, dense_scores, top_n=10):
    features = np.array([
        build_features(query, doc, b, d)
        for doc, b, d in zip(candidates, bm25_scores, dense_scores)
    ])
    scores = ltr_model.predict(features)
    ranked = sorted(zip(candidates, scores), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)
    return [doc for doc, _ in ranked[:top_n]]

LTR requires labelled training data — either explicit relevance judgements or implicit signals (click-through rate, dwell time). Building this dataset is the main cost; once available, LTR can outperform cross-encoders on domain-specific corpora because domain-specific features (date, author authority, document type) are explicitly represented.
Alternative when RRF is not available
When alpha is tuned per query type
A practical RAG system combines fast unsupervised fusion with optional reranking:

import asyncio
from sentence_transformers import CrossEncoder
from langchain_community.retrievers import BM25Retriever
from langchain_community.vectorstores import Chroma
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings

reranker = CrossEncoder("cross-encoder/ms-marco-MiniLM-L-12-v2")

async def hybrid_retrieve_and_rerank(
    query: str,
    bm25_retriever: BM25Retriever,
    chroma_store: Chroma,
    first_stage_k: int = 50,
    final_k: int = 10,
    rrf_k: int = 60,
    use_reranker: bool = True,
) -> list[dict]:
    # Stage 1: retrieve in parallel
    bm25_coro = asyncio.to_thread(bm25_retriever.invoke, query)
    dense_coro = asyncio.to_thread(
        chroma_store.similarity_search, query, k=first_stage_k
    )
    bm25_docs, dense_docs = await asyncio.gather(bm25_coro, dense_coro)

    # Stage 2: RRF fusion
    bm25_ids = [d.metadata["id"] for d in bm25_docs]
    dense_ids = [d.metadata["id"] for d in dense_docs]

    all_docs = {d.metadata["id"]: d for d in bm25_docs + dense_docs}
    fused = rrf([bm25_ids, dense_ids], k=rrf_k)           # from earlier

    # Stage 3: optional cross-encoder rerank
    candidates = [all_docs[doc_id] for doc_id, _ in fused[:first_stage_k] if doc_id in all_docs]
    if use_reranker and candidates:
        pairs = [(query, doc.page_content) for doc in candidates]
        scores = reranker.predict(pairs)
        ranked = sorted(zip(candidates, scores), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)
        candidates = [doc for doc, _ in ranked[:final_k]]
    else:
        candidates = candidates[:final_k]

    return [{"id": d.metadata["id"], "text": d.page_content} for d in candidates]

The standard metric for ranked retrieval is nDCG@K (Normalised Discounted Cumulative Gain). It accounts for the position of relevant documents — a relevant document at rank 1 is worth more than the same document at rank 10.
is the DCG of the ideal ranking. nDCG is bounded to [0, 1].
Tip 1 — Start with RRF and k=60.

It requires zero training data, no score normalisation, and delivers surprisingly good results across diverse retrieval settings. Most production deployments that measure carefully find it outperforms both individual retrievers by 5–10 nDCG points.
Tip 2 — Add a cross-encoder when precision matters more than latency.
A pretrained ms-marco or bge-reranker model (available on HuggingFace) can be added as a third stage with no labelled data. Run it on the top-20 RRF candidates to keep latency bounded.
CombMNZ's overlap bonus is almost always beneficial when combining complementary retrievers (BM25 + dense). The cost is identical; the typical gain is 1–3 nDCG points.
Tip 4 — Do not tune alpha without labels.
Picking alpha=0.5 as a default and calling it tuned is a common error. Run a proper evaluation (50+ queries with binary or graded relevance) before changing from the RRF default.
BM25 and dense retrieval are independent — run them concurrently. RRF and reranking are cheap sequential steps on the small candidate set. The critical path is
Applying fusion to identical result sets.

If your BM25 and dense retrievers both search the same 10 documents (e.g. after a pre-filter reduces the corpus to a tiny set), fusion adds no value and can hurt. Make sure each retriever genuinely sees a large candidate pool.
Using raw BM25 scores in CombSUM without normalisation.
BM25 scores can range from 0 to 50+ while cosine distances are in [-1, 1]. Without per-query min-max or z-score normalisation, BM25 dominates regardless of the intended weight.
Running a cross-encoder over the top-200 documents takes 4–8× as long as running it over the top-50. Restrict the reranker input to the first-stage top-K that fits your latency budget.
In CombSUM/CombMNZ, a document absent from retriever
, not be excluded from the combined result. Implementations that only track the intersection miss the long-tail documents that appear in just one list.
Evaluating only on the queries that drove development.
Fusion gains vary by query type. Always evaluate on a held-out set representative of production traffic; optimising on development queries overfits the fusion weights.
) is the standard unsupervised fusion algorithm.
It requires no score normalisation, is robust to outliers, and is the default in Weaviate, Milvus, and LangChain's EnsembleRetriever.
CombMNZ explicitly rewards overlap between retrievers
by multiplying the summed normalised scores by the number of retrievers that returned the document.
Score-based methods (CombSUM, CombMNZ, weighted blend) require per-query normalisation.
Min-max normalisation is simple but outlier-sensitive; z-score is more robust.
Cross-encoder rerankers (ms-marco, ColBERT) add 5–15 nDCG points
by scoring the full query–document pair, at the cost of 50–200 ms GPU latency per query.
LTR (gradient-boosted trees on feature vectors) is the best option when labelled data is available
and domain-specific signals (date, authority, type) matter.
Always run BM25 and dense retrieval in parallel
and serialise only the cheap fusion + optional reranking on the small merged candidate set.
